tossups.question: The speaker of this poem observes that just as a fruit “changes into delight through dissolution” when it is eaten, “so to my melted soul the heavens declare / All bounds transfigured into a boundless air.” This poem states that “Love” and “hatred of self” are equally valid names for “the real-flesh eater, worm unanswerable,” about which the speaker reveals: “Life is his meat, and I am still his host.” At the start of this poem, its speaker takes a break from thought to luxuriate in the (*) “long vistas of celestial calm!” The speaker of this poem writes that “the wave / Dares to explode out of the rocks in reeking / Spray” in its twenty-fourth and final stanza, just after dramatically declaring that: “The wind is rising! … We must try to live!” This poem is set by “this quiet roof, where dove-sails saunter by.” For 10 points, name this poetic meditation spurred by a coastal burial place, by Paul Valery.
tossups.answer: “The Graveyard by the Sea” [or “The Cemetery by the Sea”; or “Le cimetière marin”]
tossups.answer_sanitized: "The Graveyard by the Sea" [or "The Cemetery by the Sea"; or "Le cimetiere marin"]
tossups.question_sanitized: The speaker of this poem observes that just as a fruit "changes into delight through dissolution" when it is eaten, "so to my melted soul the heavens declare / All bounds transfigured into a boundless air." This poem states that "Love" and "hatred of self" are equally valid names for "the real-flesh eater, worm unanswerable," about which the speaker reveals: "Life is his meat, and I am still his host." At the start of this poem, its speaker takes a break from thought to luxuriate in the (*) "long vistas of celestial calm!" The speaker of this poem writes that "the wave / Dares to explode out of the rocks in reeking / Spray" in its twenty-fourth and final stanza, just after dramatically declaring that: "The wind is rising! ... We must try to live!" This poem is set by "this quiet roof, where dove-sails saunter by." For 10 points, name this poetic meditation spurred by a coastal burial place, by Paul Valery.

bonuses.leadin: In Stephen Spender’s translation, the speaker of this poem dramatically evokes the “bitter, dark, and echoing reservoir” that hides “intimations of my secret power” within his heart. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this poem set in a town on the Mediterranean coast that discusses a “worm unanswerable.” The final stanza of this poem proclaims, “The wind is rising… we must try to live!”

The author of “The Graveyard by the Sea,” Paul Valéry, claimed that “all known literature is written in the language of common sense,” except for that of this fellow Symbolist poet of A Season in Hell.

This type of character calls the universe “a defect in the purity of non-being” in a Valéry poem that Yvor Winters praised as the greatest ever. The speaker of Valéry’s “The Young Fate” is awoken by one of these characters.
answers: “The Graveyard by the Sea” [or “The Cemetery by the Sea”; or “Le cimetière marin”]
answers_sanitized: "The Graveyard by the Sea" [or "The Cemetery by the Sea"; or "Le cimetiere marin"]

parts_sanitized: Name this poem set in a town on the Mediterranean coast that discusses a "worm unanswerable." The final stanza of this poem proclaims, "The wind is rising... we must try to live!"

The author of "The Graveyard by the Sea," Paul Valery, claimed that "all known literature is written in the language of common sense," except for that of this fellow Symbolist poet of A Season in Hell.

This type of character calls the universe "a defect in the purity of non-being" in a Valery poem that Yvor Winters praised as the greatest ever. The speaker of Valery's "The Young Fate" is awoken by one of these characters.

bonuses.leadin: Answer some questions on a shared name in European literature, for 10 points each:
parts: This character discusses topics like his attempts to quit smoking with the psychoanalyst Dr. S in a novel by Italo Svevo titled for his “Confessions”.
The phrase ‘Zeno of Elea’ is used as a code word in the first chapter of this other Italian novelist’s If on a Winter’s Night a Traveller.
This Paul Valéry poem addresses the ‘cruel philosopher Zeno.’ It is set near a ‘quiet roof where the dove-sails saunter by’ and its last stanza declares that ‘we must try to live’.
answers_sanitized: "The Graveyard by the Sea" (or "Le cimetiere marin")
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Answer some questions on a shared name in European literature, for 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: This character discusses topics like his attempts to quit smoking with the psychoanalyst Dr. S in a novel by Italo Svevo titled for his "Confessions".
The phrase 'Zeno of Elea' is used as a code word in the first chapter of this other Italian novelist's If on a Winter's Night a Traveller.
This Paul Valery poem addresses the 'cruel philosopher Zeno.' It is set near a 'quiet roof where the dove-sails saunter by' and its last stanza declares that 'we must try to live'.

bonuses.leadin: This author wrote the line “The paths of glory lead but to the grave.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this writer who described “Some mute inglorious Milton” and “Some Cromwell guiltless of his country’s blood” in his poem “Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard.”
This poem, by a different author, begins and ends with images of a quiet roof and dove sails and asks the “cruel philosopher Zeno, / Have you then pierced me with your feathered arrow.”
answers: “The Graveyard By The Sea” [or reasonable translations for “Le cimetiere marin”] [by Paul Valery]
answers_sanitized: "The Graveyard By The Sea" [or reasonable translations for "Le cimetiere marin"] [by Paul Valery]
